coredata.dc:title: Transformation of medical plastic waste to valuable carbon dots: A sustainable recycling of medical waste to efficient fluorescent marker
coredata.dc:creator: Kumari, Manisha
Chaudhary, Ganga Ram
Chaudhary, Savita
coredata.dc:description: The dumped medical waste including disposable syringes, gloves and face masks is one of the primary responsible factors causing environmental threats and escalating landfills and CO2 discharges. Thus transforming these medical wastes into worthy carbon-based nanomaterials has offered an effective option for sustainable recycling of medical waste to efficient fluorescent markers. The current work utilizes disposable syringes, gloves and face masks as precursor sources to produce three different types of carbon dots (CDs) by using a simple heating method. The prepared CDs from three different sources have displayed considerable variations in the optical and luminescence properties of CDs. The supremacy of the prepared particles on biocompatibility parameters and the relationships between precursor source and concentration of formed CDs are also discussed via studying the antioxidant and seed germination assay. The outlook of controllable up cycled medical waste derived CDs has further been tested to develop selective and sensitive turn-off sensor for the detection of iron (Fe3+) ions. The corresponding quenching behavior in the presence of Fe3+ ions has further been explored by carrying out the optical and emission as a function of concentration and interference studies. The linear ranges for Fe3+ detection were 50 μM to 30 nM with a limit of detection down to 0.80 nM, 0.42 nM and 0.29 nM for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs generated from face masks, syringes and gloves respectively. This current work presents an effective alternative to the discovery of efficient methodologies for converting disposable medical waste for environmental remediation applications and progressive conclusions for accomplishing lucrative means to manage medical waste to value added products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Medical waste
Carbon dots
Quenching
Upcycling
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from medical waste
Examining the effect of CDs on the development of Vigna radiate seeds
Optical, emission and thermal characteristics of prepared CDs
Effect of CDs on seed germination and seedling growth

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Manisha Kumari is thankful to Haryana State Council for Science and Technology , Panchkula for financial support under HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476 . Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [ HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476 ], DST Chandigarh [ S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(19-20)/Sanc/12/2019/1878-1885 ], and [ S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(22-23)/Sanc/09/2022/907-915 ] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Ganga Ram is thankful to DST, SERB/F/8171/2015-16 and UGC (F. No. 194-2/2016 IC) for providing financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation showing the fabrication of medical waste-derived CDs with (b-d) HRTEM micrographs, (e) XRD profile and (f) FTIR spectra of F@CDs, G@CDs and S@CDs respectively.
(a) UV–vis. absorption spectrum and (b) Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission profiles of medical waste-derived CDs.

The corresponding effect of (a-c) pH, (d-f) ionic strength on the photo-luminescent intensity of synthesized particles medical waste-derived CDs. Along with the effect of various concentrations of CDs on (g) anti-oxidative activities and (h) hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging activities respectively.

Pictorial representation of Seed germination behaviour of Vigna radiate seeds in presence of different concentration of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs. Effect of chosen different concentrations of three kinds of synthesized CDs on (b) root length, (c) percentage inhibition growth, (d) germination percentage and (e) germination index percentage values for Vigna radiate seeds.
Fluorescence emission spectrum of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs in presence of (a-c) different chosen metal ions and as a function of (d-f) various concentrations [Fe3+] ions.
(a–c) Fluorescence spectrum of synthesized CDs representing the interference studies of Fe3+ in presence of different metal ions for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively.
(a–c) Percentage recovery of Fe3+ion from spiked samples of deionized water, tap water, lake water and rainwater in the presence of synthesized CDs.

head.ce:title: Transformation of medical plastic waste to valuable carbon dots: A sustainable recycling of medical waste to efficient fluorescent marker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dumped medical waste including disposable syringes, gloves and face masks is one of the primary responsible factors causing environmental threats and escalating landfills and CO2 discharges. Thus transforming these medical wastes into worthy carbon-based nanomaterials has offered an effective option for sustainable recycling of medical waste to efficient fluorescent markers. The current work utilizes disposable syringes, gloves and face masks as precursor sources to produce three different types of carbon dots (CDs) by using a simple heating method. The prepared CDs from three different sources have displayed considerable variations in the optical and luminescence properties of CDs. The supremacy of the prepared particles on biocompatibility parameters and the relationships between precursor source and concentration of formed CDs are also discussed via studying the antioxidant and seed germination assay. The outlook of controllable up cycled medical waste derived CDs has further been tested to develop selective and sensitive turn-off sensor for the detection of iron (Fe3+) ions. The corresponding quenching behavior in the presence of Fe3+ ions has further been explored by carrying out the optical and emission as a function of concentration and interference studies. The linear ranges for Fe3+ detection were 50 μM to 30 nM with a limit of detection down to 0.80 nM, 0.42 nM and 0.29 nM for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs generated from face masks, syringes and gloves respectively. This current work presents an effective alternative to the discovery of efficient methodologies for converting disposable medical waste for environmental remediation applications and progressive conclusions for accomplishing lucrative means to manage medical waste to value added products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The versatility of using single use plastics including surgical masks, gloves, syringes and PPE kits in health care venues such as hospitals, primary care clinics, labs, morgues, autopsy centers, laboratories, blood banks and nursing homes has shown an exponential increase of bio-hazardous waste on landfills [1–5]. The overuse of single use plastic during the pandemic time had broken all records and caused serious commotion in managing plastic waste disposal and generated universal concerns among researchers [6–8]. Furthermore, the improper collection of single use plastic waste from hospital waste is one of the primary factors of transmission risk among humans. As a result, proper waste processing and disposal of medical plastic waste is necessary for human well-being [9–12]. Up to now, different types of methods have been initiated by government agencies, such as pyrolysis, shredding, chemical treatment and physical segregation have been employed for the recovery, reusing and recycling of single used plastic waste. Previously, there have been several reports on recycling single used plastic materials in prudent manners such as for making roads, fuel production and many more. The fabrication of biodegradable surfactant from used polyethylene bags was reported by Liu et al. through the catalytic conversion method [13]. Balaji et al. have prepared the Na-ion capacitors of Al2O3 via recycling medical waste [14]. Singh and Sharma have explained the integrated means to manage plastic waste for the societal benefits [15]. Mohan et al. have developed an effective methodology to reuse contaminated PPEkits in the construction sector. The thermal conductivity and compression factors were compared and the results showed the higher scope of prepared composites for making effective thermal insulating materials [16]. However, the deterioration of plastic properties during the recycling process has restricted the quality and life span of product. Meanwhile, the recyclability of plastic waste generated from hospitals has posed the risk of bacterial and virus infection among living beings. Thus an effective and safe solution needs to be developed to handle the plastic menace specific to medical waste in an economical and eco-friendly manner.

Being a rich source of carbon, these single used plastic wastes from hospitals act as reliable precursor sources to prepare value-added fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) [17]. The proposed methodology not only tackle the pollution issue caused by single used plastic waste but is also able to provide an effective mean to convert waste to highly advanced nanoparticles which can further be used for environmental remediation applications.

With this background, we are here to propose an effective, simple and eco-friendly process to convert dumped medical waste including disposable syringes, gloves and face masks to highly fluorescent CDs with optimized optical and emission properties. Additionally, the utilization of temperature range above 100 °C in this proposed work has the ability to inactivate the transmission risk to living being, thus attaining the target of utilizing medical waste in a safer and secure manner. The biocompatibility of produced CDs was assessed by analyzing the seed germination assay with a combination of anti-oxidant activities from reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. The fluorescent properties of prepared CDs have further been employed for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ion in aqueous media. Being a cofactor for hemoproteins, a redox component in the electron transport chain, and an important component of non-haem iron enzymes, the sensing of Fe3+ ion by using biocompatible CDs prepared from single used plastic waste derived from hospitals holds great relevance in pathological and medical diagnostic purposes. In comparison to the traditional sensing methodologies for Fe3+ ions, the current work offers superiority over the photostability, biocompatibility and no requirement of additional surface passivation of chosen fluorophore for Fe3+ sensing. In addition, the sensing aptitude of prepared CDs has further been explored including tap water, rainwater and lake water. The current methodology has curtained the sensing response time for Fe3+ ions. The work offers an easy, economical and environment friendly processing means to convert plastic waste and possesses eminent potential in the arena of ecological safety.

Single use medical waste including face masks, gloves and syringes was collected from the sector 15, Chandigarh, India. Deionized water was used as a dispersion media for all the sample preparation. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and ethanol were purchased from Fisher Scientific having 99 % purity. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and Ascorbic acid were procured from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. Ammonium molybdate, sodium sulphate, iron chloride, potassium ferricyanide, quinine sulfate dye and trichloro acetic acid were acquired from Sigma Aldrich with 99 % purity. The seeds of mung beans (Vigna radiata) were bought from the local market of Sector 14, Chandigarh, India. The nitrate salts of different metal cations such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich with 99 % purity. There was no additional purification done to the utilized chemical reagents before usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQDs from medical waste
ce:section.ce:para:
The disposable waste face masks, gloves and syringes were initially collected and sanitized before use. The recycling of the respective plastic waste was performed using thermal calcination followed by the hydrothermal procedure. For the synthesis, the face masks, gloves and syringes were placed in silica crucibles and heated for 3 h in a muffle furnace at a constant moderate temperature of 250 °C. The obtained calcinated samples were made to dissolve in 20 mL of deionized water and afterward, placed in a 25 mL Teflon reactor stainless steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 7 h. Subsequently, the three different kinds of carbonized solutions were centrifuged at 7700 rpm for 20 min in order to get rid of solid residues (Fig. 1
a). After that, a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000 Da was stocked with the filtered dispersion and allowed to dialysis for 24 h. The resulting supernatants were further purified using ultracentrifugation into the solid extract of F@CDs, G@CDs and S@CDs (i.e. CDs obtained from face masks, gloves and syringes respectively) and further reserved at room temperature for the analysis.

The adsorption spectrum of developed CDs has been evaluated utilizing JASCO V-750 UV–visible spectroscopy in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence emission response of synthesized CDs was obtained using Perkin Elmer’s LS 55 instrument. The thermal insulated course for the production of CDs was carried out in an AICIL muffle furnace. The stirring of samples has been done using an IKA C-MAG HS7 magnetic stirrer with a heating plate. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of produced CDs was observed using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Perkin Elmer (RX1) equipment in the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) has been used to examine the direct imaging of atomic structure and morphological alterations in CDs. A Panalytical X’Pert Pro powder X-ray diffractometer was employed to examine the nanocrystalline structure of CDs between 5 and 90° at a scan rate of 2 deg. min−1 under monochromatic CuKα radiation. The thermal characteristics of CDs have been explored with Differential Scanning Calorimeter and Mettler Toledo (Q20) device. A digital pH meter from Mettler Toledo was used to analyze the significant impact of pH on synthesized particles. The value of quantum yield for synthesized CDs was determined using the standard reference method with the use of quinine sulfate as the standard solution having 54 % quantum yield as reference dye (λ = 310 nm, 0.1 M H2SO4 solution). The specific surface area, pore size distribution and pore volume of CDs have been scrutinized using a high-precision gas–vapor volumetric adsorption apparatus (MictrotracBEL Corp. & Belprep vacIII). The surface charge of the prepared particles has been assessed using a particle size analyzer (Anton Paar Litesizer 100) instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining the effect of CDs on the development of Vigna radiate seeds
ce:section.ce:para:
It is necessary to assess the potential toxicity of prepared CDs before implementing their usage in the applied applications. The potential effect of various concentrations of CDs in the range of 250 to 5000 ppm was performed on the Vigna Radiate (Mung beans) seeds. Initially, the chosen seeds were sterilized with HgCl2 solution for 3–4 min. Afterward, seeds were washed thoroughly with distilled water to eradicate any excess undesirable residues of HgCl2. Subsequently, the petri plate assay was studied to observe the emergence of seedlings and various growth parameters with different concentrations of CDs after 48 h of equilibration [18,19]. Additionally, a control system was set up for the evaluation of the standard parameters. The following equation has been used to compute the emergence percentage and germination index values for seeds exposed to CDs for 4 days:
(1)
Emergence
P e r c e n t a g e =
Out
o f
t o t a l
n u m b e r
o f
g e r m i n a t e d
s e e d s
Total
n u m b e r
o f
s e e d s
t a k e n
× 100
(2)
Germination
i n d e x =
N t
×
L t
/
N c
×
L c
× 100
where Nt is the total number of germinated seeds for all the chosen systems after 4 days of exposure, Lt corresponds to the average length of germinated seeds, Nc indicates the total number of germinated seeds for the control system and Lc stands for the average root length of germinated seeds for the control system. The following equation has been applied to determine the percentage inhibition growth of the seeds in the presence of synthesized CDs:
(3)
Percentage
I n h i b i t i o n
g r o w t h =
L c
-
L N
L C
where, LC is the average length in control sprouts and LN is the average length in the presence of CDs.

The total antioxidant activities of medical waste-derived CDs were examined for identifying their oxidation-inhibiting abilities. The respective analysis provides direct evidence for the biocompatibility of produced CDs. The propagation of free radicals in biological systems was examined using anti-oxidant activities. Therefore, the antioxidant effect of developed CDs was ensured prior to any environmental exposure for safety purposes. The total antioxidant capacity (TAC) reagent for the analysis was formulated by combining 0.6 M H2SO4 with solutions of 4 mM ammonium molybdate and 28 mM sodium sulphate. The three different kinds of CDs were then merged with methanol solution and added to 3 mL of the TAC reagent at various concentrations (400, 800 and 1200 ppm). The resulting mixture was left to incubate for 90 min at 90 °C. The measurement for the reference sample was carried out in a likewise manner with deionized water. After cooling to room temperature, the absorbance of each sample in the presence of CDs was determined at 680 nm. In the experimental process, ascorbic acid was used as the positive control [20].

Furthermore, the hydrogen peroxide assay was examined to gauge the ability of CDs to scavenge the hydrogen peroxide. A 10 mM hydrogen peroxide solution was mixed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) having 7.4 pH. Afterward, various concentrations of CDs (400, 800 and 1200 ppm) were quickly combined with 2 mL of H2O2 in PBS. The resulting solution was allowed to stand at 37 °C for 10 min. The absorbance of each sample was measured in comparison to a blank (one sample without hydrogen peroxide) [21]. The amount of hydrogen peroxide scavenging was calculated using the following equation:
(4)
H 2
O 2
scavenging
percentage =
A o
-
A C
A O
× 100
where, Ao and Ac indicate the value of absorbance for the control and sample respectively.

Additionally, the electron donor and acceptor potential of the CDs synthesized from medical waste was scrutinized using the total reduction potential assay. The evaluations of the reducing ability of developed CDs provide a clear indication of their inherent anti-oxidant ability. The analysis was carried out by combining three different concentrations of fabricated F@CDs, G@CDs and S@CDs (3–9 mg/mL) in 1 mL of deionized water. The respective prepared solution was then combined with 2.2 mL of potassium ferricyanide solution (0.2 M and pH = 6.6) and 2.2 mL of 1 % phosphate buffer solution. After allowing the solution to incubate at 50 °C for 30 min, the prepared solution was mixed with 2.5 mL of 10 % trichloroacetic acid. The resulting mixture was kept for centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 6 min. The obtained supernatant after centrifugation was combined with 0.5 mL of a solution having 0.1 % ferric chloride and 2.5 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, each of the prepared solutions was allowed to stir for 10 min. The optical densities of the relevant samples were probed at 700 nm for analysis purposes [22–24].

The standard fluorescence detection technique was utilized to assess the sensing ability of medical waste-derived CDs against a variety of chosen ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+. For the experimental purpose, the fluorescence emission profile of an incubated equi-volume solution of each chosen analyte molecule with synthesized F@CDs, G@CDs and S@CDs in an aqueous medium was recorded in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The selectivity and sensitivity of the proposed CDs-based sensor were also evaluated by adding a set concentration of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of selected metal ions.

Taking advantage of the sensing features of CDs, the practical utility in real water samples (i.e. deionized water, tap water, rainwater and lake water) was probed for the developed sensing system. The chosen samples were spiked with different concentrations of Fe3+ metal ions (20 nM to 60 nM) in presence of CDs. Before testing, prepared solutions were allowed to stand at room temperature for 2 min. The corresponding emission spectrum was obtained at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The following equation was used to determine the recovery values:
(5)
Re cov e r y
%
=
C 1
-
C R
c X

Herein, CI represents the concentration of Fe3+ in real water sources after dilution with the standard solution of Fe3+, CR corresponds to the concentration of Fe3+ in selected water samples before mixing the external standard solution of Fe3+ and Cx is the concentration of Fe3+ added in real water samples.

The surface functional groups of medical waste-derived CDs were further examined using FTIR spectroscopy as displayed in Fig. 1(f). For F@CDs, the characteristics peak at 3132 cm−1 was observed due to the presence of OH and NH2 stretching vibration. The small absorption peak at 3010 cm−1 was ascribed to the CH stretching vibration [25]. Two characteristic peaks at 1615 cm−1 and 1557 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and CC stretching vibration respectively. The strong absorption peaks located at 1401 cm−1 and 1140 cm−1 were assigned to the COOH bond and COC stretching vibration respectively. The small peak that appeared at 876 cm−1 occurred due to the CC bending vibration. For S@CDs, the strong characteristic peak observed at 3139 cm−1 has confirmed the existence of OH and NH stretching vibration in the prepared particles. The presence of CO and COOH functional groups have appeared at the wavelength of 1634 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 respectively. The absorption peak observed at 1017 cm−1 was ascribed to the COC stretching vibration in the S@CDs [26]. The small absorption peak that appeared at 670 cm−1 corresponds to the = CH functionalities in the respective sample. For G@CDs, the absorption wavelength detected at 3145 cm−1 accounts for the OH and NH stretching frequency. Two absorption wavelengths observed at the wavelength of 2857 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 were ascribed to the CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration respectively [27]. The typical characteristic peaks at 1623 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 were assigned to the CC, CO and COOH bonds present in G@CDs. The strong absorption band at 1010 cm−1 was attributed to the COC stretching vibration [28]. These outcomes proved that all the synthesized CDs had abundant hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl and amino functional groups on their surface which further enhanced the fluorescence sensing applications of CDs.

To check the stability of formed suspensions, the zeta potential is one of the primary techniques to measure the surface charge of prepared CDs. Fig. S3 (a)–(c) showed the zeta potential value of the F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs, which was found to be −16.9 mV, −14.04 mV and −19.7 mV respectively. The high value of zeta potential was due to the negatively charged functional groups such as COOH, NH2 and OH on the surface of developed particles [29,30]. Also, the high zeta potential indicated the colloidal and electrostatic stability of prepared samples. The specific surface area and porous nature of synthesized particles were investigated by N2 physio-sorption analysis in liquid nitrogen at 77 K ( Fig. S4 (a)–(f)). The adsorption temperature and saturated vapor pressure for the analysis were set to be 77 K and 100.95 kPa, respectively. The analysis confirmed that produced CDs acquired type IV adsorption–desorption isotherm with hysteresis loop. The estimated surface area for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs was measured to be 7.47 m2/g, 8.66 m2/g and 4.28 m2/g respectively. Furthermore, the respective linear fit curves demonstrated the pore diameter in the range of 3–10 nm for all three types of CDs. Specifically, the mean pore size of F@CDs was 8.54 nm while in the case of G@CDs and S@CDs, it was found to be 3.74 nm and 8.65 nm, respectively. The pore volume was determined to be 0.00159 cm3/g, 0.004015 cm3/g and 0.001873 cm3/g, respectively for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs. The obtained results confirmed that the synthesized mesoporous CDs could be even more efficient in detecting contaminants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical, emission and thermal characteristics of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis. absorption spectrum of all the sets of CDs has been illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). The strong absorption band in the UV–visible region of 220–280 nm was observed due to the n → π* transition of the CO bond and π → π* transition of the conjugated CC bond in the core structure of synthesized CDs [27]. The unpaired electrons on the oxygen atom of CO represent the non-bonding electrons. As a result, one of the unpaired oxygen electrons got excited to the anti-bonding orbital and further led to the n → π* transition. These outcomes suggested that the surface oxygenated (CO) states were introduced during the synthesis of CDs. During experimentation, it was also seen that with the increase in concentration, the absorption value increased for the developed particles. Hence, the increased CO group content can be used to explain the stronger UV absorption characteristics of the synthesized particles [31–33].

The excitation-dependent fluorescent emission spectrum has been observed in Fig. 2(b). The photo-luminescent (PL) intensities noticeably showed variations when the excitation wavelength increased from 250 to 330 nm. The emission-peak location tended to be slightly red-shifted in all three types of prepared particles. The maximum PL peak for F@CDs was detected in the wavelength range of 425–440 nm. In the case of G@CDs, the maximum PL peak was observed in the wavelength range of 380–400 nm. For the S@CDs, the maximum PL peak was observed in the 400 to 500 nm wavelength range. These kinds of PL characteristics attributes were chiefly explained via the optical selection of nanoparticles with different sizes which is known as the quantum effect, multiple defects on the surface of CDs and the band gap transitions caused by the conjugated π-domains [34,35].

The generated excitons from the carbogenic cores were easily confined at the surface defects across the exterior surface of synthesized CDs before recombination which was revealed by the slight difference that occurred between the emission maxima relative to the excitation wavelength. Moreover, the change in fluorescence intensity of synthesized CDs when the excitation wavelength increased in the range of 340 to 440 nm has been illustrated in ( Fig. S5 (a)–(c). The quantum yield value calculated using the reference standard approach was found to be as high as 61 %, 65 % and 68 % for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively in reference to the quinine sulfate dye [28]. The emission intensity and propensity for visual agglomeration were also examined to scrutinize the stability of the produced CDs for up to 30 days. The specific solutions for all three types of medical waste-derived CDs i.e. F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs have not exhibited any sort of visible aggregation in the samples and their emission behaviour was found to be unaltered during the 30-day monitoring phase. As a result, it can be inferred that formed particles derived from medical waste had a greater stability rate. The stability of prepared CDs was further investigated by altering the pH values in the suitable range of the reaction medium from 1 to 11 as depicted in Fig. 3 (a)–(c).

It was observed from the experimental result that emission intensity steadily rose between the pH range of 1 and 7 with the highest emission occurring at pH = 7. The respective outcomes confirmed the synthesized particle’s stability in the neutral media. The fluorescence intensity significantly reduced as the pH level was raised above 7 of the reaction medium. This change in the fluorescence behavior was mostly caused due to the acidic nature of CDs in basic environmental conditions. The main reason used to explain the respective results was the removal and addition of the proton (H+) component of the hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalities present on the exterior facade of CDs. This further modified the electrostatic interaction behavior of produced CDs. Additionally, the impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence potential of prepared CDs has been evaluated in the presence of various NaCl and KCl solution concentrations (0.2–0.8 M) as represented in Fig. 3(d)–(f). The resulting graphs inferred that NaCl and KCl solutions with high concentrations had negligible impact on the fluorescence emission intensity of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs. The above results have provided evidence for the stability of all three kinds of synthesized CDs in high-ionic strength conditions. These results have further established the aptness of synthesized particles in the domain of toxin sensing due to their high PL emission characteristics and remarkable stability in a wide range of physiological and ionic strength mediums [36,37].

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) techniques were utilized to assess the thermal stability of the medical waste-derived CDs. In the case of F@CDs, the sample showed three significant weight losses in the temperature range of 30 °C to 800 °C during the thermal degradation process ( Fig. S6 (a)–(c)). The loss of mass obtained below 265 °C was due to the vaporization of physio-sorbed and chemisorbed water molecules because of the presence of hydrogen bonds in F@CDs. The next two thermal losses occurred between 280 and 780 °C which were attributable to the dissolution of oxygen-containing functional moieties, oxidative disintegration and elimination of pyro-gases. Whereas for S@CDs, two major weight losses between 100 °C and 700 °C were attributed to the breakdown of water content and thermal decomposition of functional groups present on the surface of prepared particles. For G@CDs, the samples displayed a loss of mass at the temperature of 130 °C and 700 °C which was caused due to the evaporation of moisture content and breakdown of various functionalities such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups. These outcomes indicated the high thermal stability and purity of all three types of CDs synthesized from medical waste. In the DSC graphs, the glass transition temperature was identified at 70 °C, 75 °C and 68 °C for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively ( Fig. S6 (d)–(f)). The second thermal event occurred at the glass transition temperature of 314 °C, 310 °C and 308 °C for the prepared samples. These studies have further endorsed the remarkable thermal stability of synthesized CDs.

The antioxidant ability of developed CDs was examined to determine their potential as antioxidants in biological samples. For the analysis purpose, various chosen concentrations of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs in the range of 400 ppm to 1200 ppm were employed. According to the resultant data, a modest decline in the antioxidant potential of CDs was observed as the concentration increased from 400 ppm to 1200 ppm (Fig. 3(g). Moreover, the CDs derived from medical waste tended to neutralize free radical species by transferring electrons. This behavioral aspect was primarily due to the presence of hydroxyl groups which were present on the exterior surface of CDs. However, the antioxidant activity of F@CDs was marginally higher than S@CDs and G@CDs. The respective phenomenon was related to the variations in the sizes of developed CDs.

The ability of synthesized CDs to scavenge free radicals was also assessed by measuring the respective absorbance value at 290 nm. The data showed that the percentage of scavenging activity was found to be decreasing with the increase in concentration of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3(h). On the other hand, the hydroxyl functional groups present in sufficient quantities on the surface of developed CDs prevented the solutions of samples from degradation through oxidation. Therefore, the hydrogen peroxide free radical scavenging ability of developed CDs has been confirmed through these obtained results which further led to their utilization in numerous biomedical applications.

To ascertain the electron donor and acceptor characteristics of the produced CDs, the related reduction potential measurements have also been determined as shown in Fig. S7 . The decreased reduction ability of CDs served as a significant indicator of their antioxidant potential. In the analysis, the optical density values of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs were compared with ascorbic acid (the control standard). The result of the activity suggested that the absorbance value increased linearly with the increase in the chosen dosage (400 ppm, 800 ppm and 1200 ppm) of the synthesized particles. These findings further supported the strong reduction potential in the case of all three medical waste-derived CDs. The fundamental explanation for the remarkable reduction potential of the F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs was the high surface charge ratio and the existence of hydroxyl functional group which accelerated the breakdown and disintegration of weak peroxide bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on seed germination and seedling growth
ce:section.ce:para: Vigna radiate seeds were chosen as the model seed to determine whether developed CDs priming can enhance seed germination and seedling growth. Vigna radiate seeds were soaked in deionized water (control standard) and various concentrations of CDs solutions (250 to 5000 ppm). It was discovered that all of the groups of chosen seeds germinated successfully within 48 h during the respective analysis, thus observations were carried out every 6 h to compare the rate of seed germination until 2 days after sowing in petri dishes. The representative images of CDs-primed Vigna radiate seeds before and after germination in Petri dishes are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The results showed that the seeds in the presence of deionized water had the lowest rate of germination, whereas the CDs solutions at every chosen concentration, particularly high concentrations were efficient in boosting the seed germination and growth. In contrast to the control group which only had a relatively low germination rate and after 12 h of sowing, half of the Vigna radiate seeds primed with 5000 ppm CDs concentration sprouted with evident radical growth in the case of the prepared CDs. The effects of concentrations on the observational parameters such as germination %, germination index and nucleus growth inhibition have been studied in the presence of CDs. The results displayed that each seed in the chosen range of concentration was observed to have a germination percentage of almost 100 %. Additionally, the growth of nuclei in Vigna radiate seeds in the presence of CDs has been further supported by the significant increase in the values of germination index and root length. The value of average root length was observed to be equivalent in all three kinds of CDs, however, 3000 and 5000 ppm concentrations produced the maximum seedling growth.

Due to the large increase in water absorption capacity of Vigna radiate seeds, the value of the germination index was determined to be maximal in the case of 3000 ppm, which is 99.9 %. In all the virtual instances, the percentage inhibition growth (PIG) value was insignificant. In the case of high concentrations, the enhancement in biocompatibility was found to be greater than 100 % as compared to the control standard system. According to all the resultant graphs shown in Fig. 4(b)–(e), developed CDs were observed to be highly biocompatible and can be used in the agriculture sector to strengthen rootlets due to their non-toxic characteristics.

The photostability, pH and electrolyte-induced variations in the optical and emission properties of CDs not only revealed the superior stability but also demonstrated their potential utilization in environmental sensing. Since the designed sensor established remarkable biocompatibility properties, as a result, synthesized CDs were investigated for their potential aptitude as a fluorescence sensor to detect various toxic ions in aqueous solutions. The high percentage of carbon and oxygen imparted higher adsorption and emission potential to the developed sensor which aids in more effective target molecule binding. Fig. 5 (a)–(c) displayed the fluorescence variation of all the synthesized CDs solutions in the presence of several chosen metal ions. After the addition of 12 different metal ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The fluorescence variation of all the synthesized CDs solutions in the presence of Fe2+, Ag+ was illustrated in Fig. S8. The numerous different singularities in the interaction between the CDs and metal ions were observed during the experimental studies. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs were almost completely turned off in the presence of Fe3+ ions. Hence, the developed particles were effective for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

The quenching effects of Fe3+ ions were determined to be very strong, as represented in Fig. 5(a)–(c). However, no other chosen ions showed a significant quenching effect in the case of all the synthesized CDs. Therefore, the developed sensor binds selectively to Fe3+ ions in comparison to other cations, as demonstrated by experimental data. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, few negatively charged groups present over the surface of synthesized CDs formed coordinate bonds with the small-sized and highly electric-charged Fe3+ ions. This evidence further established the quenching mechanism as potentially applicable to the stable and non-fluorescent ground state complexation between CDs and Fe3+ ions. The phenomenon of rapid electron transfer excited states of the prepared CDs into vacant d-orbitals of Fe3+ ions. It is also significant to note that carboxyl and amine groups in the carbon core of F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs were enriched with excited state electrons after the radiation absorption, thereby acting as fluorescent centers [38]. The de-excitation of fluorophores caused by the light emissions was eventually reduced to an extent that depends on the added amounts of Fe3+ ions as these interact strongly with the added Fe3+ ions, relocating electron densities to the unoccupied orbitals of Fe3+ ions [39,40].

Given the outstanding optical properties of CDs in harsh environments, we examined the possibility of employing F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs as Fe3+ ions probes. Furthermore, the sensitivity of developed CDs towards Fe3+ ions has been further evaluated using the titration method. To explore the optimal detection potential of synthesized CDs, the concentration of Fe3+ ions has been varied in dynamic range from 70 μM to 30 nM under the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. It has been discovered that the strength of the emission intensity of developed CDs varied linearly on decreasing their concentration. There is a strong linear relationship between the quenching ratio [(F0-F)/F0] and various Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 70 μM to 30 nM as seen in Fig. 5 (d)–(f). The detection limit was determined to be 0.80 nM, 0.42 nM and 0.29 nM for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively which is much lower than the safe drinking level (0.3 mg/L) of Fe3+ ions demanded by the USEPA (United States Environmental Protection Agency) based on the signal-to-noise ratio of 3 [40]. A few reports on CDs probes for Fe3+ ions were mentioned in Table S2 . The reported papers have possessed various drawbacks such as the utilization of organic solvents, expensive metal ions, poor detection limits and so on. From the obtained results, it has been found that the developed CDs have reflected ultimate selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ ions with the excellent value of detection limit as compared to the other systems as reported in the literature. In summary, fabricated CDs ensured sufficiently low detection limits and excellent sensitivity to Fe3+ in the aqueous system.

The selectivity is a crucial factor in determining the applicability of fluorescent probes. The selectivity of prepared F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs was evaluated for Fe3+ and several interfering ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ at the final concentration of 50 μM as presented in Fig. 6 (a)–(c). After the addition of several chosen ions, the results demonstrated that only Fe3+ significantly quenches the fluorescence emission of synthesized CDs. Hereby, the synthesized CDs have remarkable selectivity for the detection of Fe3+ in practical applications involving the presence of common interfering ions. Furthermore, the quenching process of the proposed sensor has been verified using Stern-Volmer analysis [38].
(5)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q
In this case, F0 stands for the initial fluorescence intensity without a quencher and F stands for the fluorescence intensity with a quencher. Q represents the number of Fe3+ ions in the solution and Ksv stands for Stern-Volmer association constant. The following standard deviation rule was used to determine the detection limit value:
(6)
LOD = 3 SD / P
Here, SD denotes the standard deviation of the sample and P stands for the slope of the calibration curve ( Fig. S9 (a)–(c)). The detection limit of the developed sensor was found to be 0.80 nM, 0.42 nM and 0.29 nM for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively.

In order to assess the validity and practical applicability of developed CDs, standard addition test was utilized in ambient water samples such as tap water, lake water, rain water and deionized water collected from the local premises around Chandigarh. The obtained real water samples were spiked with various concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 20 nM–60 nM. Further, the effectiveness of the designed sensor has also been assessed using fluorescence spectroscopy in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm (λex. = 310 nm). From the resultant emission profile, it has been indicated that the intensity of developed CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions has been augmented in the presence of chosen real water samples which has further proved the potential efficacy of the prepared system. The recovery percentage was determined to be in the range of 92–96 % (Fig. 7 (a)–(c)). The obtained results have been found to be in good agreement with the label claim for Fe3+ ions content. It has been concluded that the developed F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs have shown superior sensitivity as well as selectivity toward Fe3+ ions even in the case of real water sources. The current studies have further enhanced the scope of synthesized medical waste-derived CDs in biomedical field for the detection of Fe3+ ions with great precision and accuracy.

The current article adopted an economical, eco-friendly and facile method to develop CDs with sizes less than 10 nm via recycling of medical solid waste (i.e. face masks, gloves and syringes) through thermochemical conversion followed by a hydrothermal process. Further, relevant characterization techniques have been employed to verify the structural and physicochemical properties of CDs. The developed three kinds of CDs possessed excellent photo-stability, colloidal solubility and high stability in various environmental conditions. The developed CDs exhibited exceptional PL activity with excitation-dependent emission and possessed rich functionalities over their surface like COOH, NH2 and OH to enhance their unique optical properties. Interestingly, the formed CDs have also demonstrated higher bio-compatibility examined by employing total antioxidant activity. For the first time, medical waste-derived CDs were used as a seed nano-priming agent for the cultivation of Vigna radiate seeds and were observed to have positive impacts on promoting seed germination and seedling development. The developed nano-sensor was productively implemented for Fe3+ ions fluorescence sensing via quenching of fluorescence intensity which established them as a capable sensor for Fe3+ analysis. The linear range of experimentation was determined to be 50 μM to 30 nM and the detection limit was as low as 0.80 nM, 0.42 nM and 0.29 nM for F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs respectively. Using the developed CDs, the determination of Fe3+ in various kinds of real water sources accomplishes satisfactory outcomes. The amine, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the synthesized F@CDs, S@CDs and G@CDs could sensitively link with Fe3+ and form the stable and non-fluorescent ground state complexation between CDs and Fe3+ ions. The produced CDs showed several advantages via a rapid fluorescence response including great stability, biocompatibility, superior sensitivity and selectivity, low interference as well as a broad linear range of Fe3+ detection and ease of production.

Manisha Kumari: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Ganga Ram Chaudhary: Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Manisha Kumari is thankful to Haryana State Council for Science and Technology, Panchkula for financial support under HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476. Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(19-20)/Sanc/12/2019/1878-1885], and [S&T&RE/RP/147/e-2873/(22-23)/Sanc/09/2022/907-915] and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Ganga Ram is thankful to DST, SERB/F/8171/2015-16 and UGC (F. No. 194-2/2016 IC) for providing financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.123910.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution with high selectivity and sensitivity by using sulfasalazine functionalized microgels
